message:
perf(channel): migrate broadcast, mpsc, and watch channels from std to parking_lot locks

Completes the parking_lot migration for all three channel implementations,
removing lock-poisoning ceremony and the associated dead-code test suites.

broadcast.rs:
- std::sync::Mutex → parking_lot::Mutex for channel inner state
- Removes lock_recover() poison-recovery helper (module-level) and
  recover_lock() test helper — parking_lot never poisons
- All .lock().expect("broadcast lock poisoned") → .lock()
- Removes 5 poison-specific tests that validated graceful behavior when
  std Mutex was poisoned during Drop (sender_drop_on_poisoned_mutex,
  receiver_drop_on_poisoned_mutex, recv_drop_on_poisoned_mutex, and
  their _still_updates variants). These tests are inapplicable to
  parking_lot which never enters a poisoned state.

mpsc.rs:
- std::sync::Mutex → parking_lot::Mutex for channel inner state
- Removes lock_recover() poison-recovery helper and recover_lock() test helper
- All .lock().expect("channel lock poisoned") → .lock()
- Removes 8 poison-specific tests (~205 lines): sender_drop, receiver_drop,
  reserve_future_drop, send_permit_drop, and their _still_updates variants.
  All tested std::sync::Mutex poison recovery which is not needed with
  parking_lot.

watch.rs:
- std::sync::{Mutex, RwLock, RwLockReadGuard} → parking_lot equivalents
- All .read()/.write().expect("watch lock poisoned") → direct calls
- wake_all_waiters() and Sender::Drop: removes if-let-Ok poison guard
  patterns, replaced with direct .lock() calls
- Receiver::Drop: removes if-let-Ok guard, directly locks and retains
- Removes 1 poison test (send_and_modify_on_poisoned_waiters_mutex)

Total: ~400 lines of poison-recovery code and tests removed across all
three channel types with no behavioral changes to channel semantics.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
